scene_title: The same. Before the walls of Harfleur

lines.speaker: BARDOLPH
lines.text: On, on, on, on, on! to the breach, to the breach!

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: Pray thee, corporal, stay: the knocks are too hot; and, for mine

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: own part, I have not a case of lives: the humour of

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: it is too hot, that is the very plainsong of it.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: The plain-song is most just; for humours do abound:

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Knocks go and come; God’s vassals drop and die;

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: And sword and shield,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: In bloody field,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Doth win immortal fame.

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: Would I were in an alehouse in London! I would give all

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: my fame for a pot of ale and safety.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: And I:

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: If wishes would prevail with me,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: My purpose should not fail with me,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: But thither would I hie.

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: As duly, but not as truly,

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: As bird doth sing on bough.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Up to the breach, you dogs! avaunt, you cullions! Driving them forward.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Be merciful, great duke, to men of mould.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Abate thy rage, abate thy manly rage,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Abate thy rage, great duke!

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Good bawcock, bate thy rage; use lenity, sweet chuck!

lines.speaker: NYM
lines.text: These be good humours! your honour wins bad humours.

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: As young as I am, I have observ’d these three swashers. I

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: am boy to them all three: but all they three, though they

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: would serve me, could not be man to me; for indeed three

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: such antics do not amount to a man. For Bardolph, he is

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: white-livered and red-faced; by the means whereof a’ faces it out, but

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: fights not. For Pistol, he hath a killing tongue and a quiet

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: sword; by the means whereof a’ breaks words, and keeps whole weapons.

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: For Nym, he hath heard that men of few words are the

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: best men; and therefore he scorns to say his prayers, lest a’

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: should be thought a coward: but his few bad words are matched

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: with as few good deeds; for a’ never broke any man’s head

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: but his own, and that was against a post when he was

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: drunk. They will steal anything, and call it purchase. Bardolph stole a

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: lute-case, bore it twelve leagues, and sold it for three half-pence. Nym

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: and Bardolph are sworn brothers in filching, and in Calais they stole

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: a fire-shovel: I knew by that piece of service the men would

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: carry coals. They would have me as familiar with men’s pockets as

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: their gloves or their handkerchers: which makes much against my manhood, if

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: I should take from another’s pocket to put into mine; for it

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: is plain pocketing up of wrongs. I must leave them, and seek

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: some better service: their villainy goes against my weak stomach, and therefore

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: I must cast it up.

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: Captain Fluellen, you must come presently to the mines; the Duke of

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: Gloucester would speak with you.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: To the mines! tell you the duke, it is not so good

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: to come to the mines; for, look you, the mines is not

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: according to the disciplines of the war: the concavities of it is

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: not sufficient; for, look you, th’ athversary, you may discuss unto the

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: duke, look you, is digt himself four yard under the countermines: by

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Cheshu, I think a’ will plow up all, if there is not

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: better directions.

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: The Duke of Gloucester, to whom the order of the siege is

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: given, is altogether directed by an Irishman, a very valiant gentleman, i’

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: faith.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: It is Captain Macmorris, is it not?

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: I think it be.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: By Cheshu, he is an ass, as in the world: I will

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: verify as much in his beard: he has no more directions in

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: the true disciplines of the wars, look you, of the Roman disciplines,

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: than is a puppy-dog.

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: Here a’ comes; and the Scots captain, Captain Jamy, with him.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Captain Jamy is a marvellous falorous gentleman, that is certain; and of

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: great expedition and knowledge in th’ aunchient wars, upon my particular knowledge

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: of his directions: by Cheshu, he will maintain his argument as well

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: as any military man in the world, in the disciplines of the

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: pristine wars of the Romans.

lines.speaker: JAMY
lines.text: I say gud-day, Captain Fluellen.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: God-den to your worship, good Captain James.

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: How now, Captain Macmorris! have you quit the mines? have the pioneers given o’er?

lines.speaker: MACMORRIS
lines.text: By Chrish, la! tish ill done: the work ish give over, the

lines.speaker: MACMORRIS
lines.text: trompet sound the retreat. By my hand, I swear, and my father’s

lines.speaker: MACMORRIS
lines.text: soul, the work ish ill done; it ish give over: I would

lines.speaker: MACMORRIS
lines.text: have blowed up the town, so Chrish save me, la! in an

lines.speaker: MACMORRIS
lines.text: hour: O, tish ill done, tish ill done; by my hand, tish

lines.speaker: MACMORRIS
lines.text: ill done!

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Captain Macmorris, I beseech you now, will you voutsafe me, look you,

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: a few disputations with you, as partly touching or concerning the disciplines

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: of the war, the Roman wars, in the way of argument, look

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: you, and friendly communication; partly to satisfy my opinion, and partly for

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: the satisfaction, look you, of my mind, as touching the direction of

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: the military discipline; that is the point.

lines.speaker: JAMY
lines.text: It sall be vary gud, gud feith, gud captains bath: and I

lines.speaker: JAMY
lines.text: sall quit you with gud leve, as I may pick occasion; that

lines.speaker: JAMY
lines.text: sall I, marry.

lines.speaker: MACMORRIS
lines.text: It is no time to discourse, so Chrish save me: the day

lines.speaker: MACMORRIS
lines.text: is hot, and the weather, and the wars, and the king, and

lines.speaker: MACMORRIS
lines.text: the dukes: it is no time to discourse. The town is beseeched,

lines.speaker: MACMORRIS
lines.text: and the trumpet call us to the breach; and we talk, and,

lines.speaker: MACMORRIS
lines.text: be Chrish, do nothing: ’tis shame for us all: so God sa’

lines.speaker: MACMORRIS
lines.text: me, ’tis shame to stand still; it is shame, by my hand:

lines.speaker: MACMORRIS
lines.text: and there is throats to be cut, and works to be done;

lines.speaker: MACMORRIS
lines.text: and there ish nothing done, so Chrish sa’ me, la!

lines.speaker: JAMY
lines.text: By the mess, ere theise eyes of mine take themselves to slomber,

lines.speaker: JAMY
lines.text: ay’ll de gud service, or ay’ll lig i’ the grund for it;

lines.speaker: JAMY
lines.text: ay, or go to death; and ay’ll pay’t as valorously as I

lines.speaker: JAMY
lines.text: may, that sall I suerly do, that is the breff and the

lines.speaker: JAMY
lines.text: long. Marry, I wad full fain heard some question ’tween you tway.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Captain Macmorris, I think, look you, under your correction, there is not

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: many of your nation—

lines.speaker: MACMORRIS
lines.text: Of my nation! What ish my nation? Ish a villain, and a

lines.speaker: MACMORRIS
lines.text: bastard, and a knave, and a rascal—What ish my nation? Who talks

lines.speaker: MACMORRIS
lines.text: of my nation?

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Look you, if you take the matter otherwise than is meant, Captain

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Macmorris, peradventure I shall think you do not use me with that

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: affability as in discretion you ought to use me, look you; being

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: as good a man as yourself, both in the disciplines of war,

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: and in the derivation of my birth, and in other particularities.

lines.speaker: MACMORRIS
lines.text: I do not know you so good a man as myself: so

lines.speaker: MACMORRIS
lines.text: Chrish save me, I will cut off your head.

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: Gentlemen both, you will mistake each other.

lines.speaker: JAMY
lines.text: A! that’s a foul fault. A parley sounded.

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: The town sounds a parley.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Captain Macmorris, when there is more better opportunity to be required, look

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: you, I will be so bold as to tell you I know

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: the disciplines of war; and there is an end.

stage_directions.text: Enter Nym, Bardolph, Pistol and Boy.

glosses.definition: blows; cannon-shots
a pair of lives — Nym complains he has only one
the unembellished melody; the plain truth — musical metaphor for bluntness
God's subjects — soldiers as servants of providence
a London pub — the Boy's wish for home and safety
hurry; go quickly
begone! get out of here!
rascals; base fellows (also a crude term for testicles)
men of earth; mortal clay — Pistol's inflated plea
fine fellow (from French 'beau coq' — fine cock)
mildness; mercy
term of endearment — 'chuck' = chicken, dear
swaggering braggarts
clowns; grotesques
cowardly — a pale liver was thought to indicate cowardice
brazens it out with his face; bluffs

glosses.phrase: killing tongue and a quiet sword
glosses.definition: deadly in speech, silent in arms — all bluster

glosses.phrase: breaks words, and keeps whole weapons
glosses.definition: uses up words, not weapons
theft renamed as acquisition
instrument-case — Bardolph's petty theft
about 36 miles — a league is roughly 3 miles
English-held port on the French Channel coast from 1347–1558
a shovel for moving coals on a hearth — pettiest possible theft
endure indignity — proverbial for demeaning work (same as R&J 1.1)
tucking away of grievances — pun on literal pocket-picking
vomit; throw it up — the Boy rejects the thieves' company
immediately; right now
tunnels dug beneath siege walls to collapse them
Fluellen's pedantic catchphrase — the classical 'military science' he keeps citing
the hollow excavations — the mine-tunnels
the adversary, in Fluellen's Welsh accent — 'th' for 'the'; dialect marker, not a typo
dug himself — Welsh-dialect form, preserved as characterization
counter-tunnels dug to intercept enemy mines
Fluellen's Welsh oath 'Jesu' — 'Cheshu' = Jesus; dialect marker throughout
Captain Macmorris — Shakespeare's only Irish character; the four-captains scene stages the emerging British Isles identity
the Irish captain; his accent ('ish' for 'is') is Shakespeare's only extended Irish dialect
valorous, in Fluellen's pronunciation
ancient — Fluellen's pronunciation; used as noun = ensign (lowest officer)
the wars of ancient Rome — Fluellen's obsession with classical military theory
'good day' in Jamy's Scottish accent — another dialect marker
'good-even', contracted — Fluellen's return greeting
military engineers who dig trenches and tunnels
'by Christ' in Macmorris's Irish pronunciation — dialect marker
'it is ill done' — 'tish' = 'tis, with Irish intonation
trumpet (dialect spelling)
vouchsafe; grant — Fluellen's pronunciation
Fluellen's comic formality — he is asking to quarrel theoretically
shall, in Jamy's Scottish accent
'good faith' — Jamy's Scottish oath
by the mass — mild Scottish oath
'I'll do good service' — Jamy's Scots
lie in the ground — Scottish dialect for 'die'
would very much like to have heard — Scottish dialect
two — Scottish dialect
Macmorris's defensive outburst — the play's most-discussed Irish line; a challenge to Fluellen's presumption about 'his nation'
perhaps
friendly courtesy
pedigree; ancestry
Macmorris's blunt threat — the captains' scholarly dispute collapses into violence
trumpet-call for a negotiation truce — signals the Harfleur governor's offer to talk
Fluellen's dignified close — 'that's that'

annotations.title: The Boy's soliloquy — the war seen from below
annotations.body: The Boy's long speech assessing Pistol, Bardolph, and Nym is one of the play's most important counter-frames. The Chorus has just shown us 'our swift scene' flying 'with imagined wing' to Harfleur; Henry has just delivered the breach oration. The Boy, aged perhaps twelve, walks onstage and gives us an adult appraisal: the three older men who are his masters are cowards, thieves, and frauds. The speech is clear-eyed ('I must leave them, and seek some better service'); it is also delivered directly to the audience, without irony. Later, at 4.4 and 4.7, the Boy will be revealed to have been left with the camp's luggage and killed by the French during their raid. The play thus frames the war with the perspective of an intelligent child whom the play allows to speak truly once, and then allows to die. Readers should notice whom Shakespeare trusts with clarity and whom he rewards with survival.
sources: C. L. Barber, Shakespeare's Festive Comedy (1959)
Thomas Healy, Shakespeare, Alienation and the Early Modern Stage (1994)

annotations.title: The four captains — a British Isles tableau, under stress
annotations.body: This is Shakespeare's four-captains scene: Gower (English), Fluellen (Welsh), Jamy (Scottish), and Macmorris (Irish) serving together before Harfleur. It stages the emerging idea of a British Isles identity united in foreign war — the nationalist premise that would later underwrite the Stuart union of crowns. But the scene does not smooth the union. Fluellen's classical pedantry, Jamy's Scots-cadenced courtesy, and Macmorris's explosive defensiveness — 'What ish my nation? Who talks of my nation?' — stage the identity as contested at the moment of its formation. Macmorris is Shakespeare's only Irish character in the canon, and this is the most-discussed Irish line in Renaissance drama. It can be read as comic (an Irishman over-reacting to a routine Welsh observation) or as politically serious (an Irishman interrupting the construction of 'Britishness' to demand whose nation is being talked about). Modern productions and critics (especially post-1980 Irish scholarship) tend to the latter. The scene's unresolved tension — the parley sounds and the captains have to abandon their dispute — is the point: British identity is assembled under the pressure of a common French enemy, and unresolved by the assembly.
sources: David Cairns & Shaun Richards, Writing Ireland (1988)
Philip Edwards, Threshold of a Nation (1979)
Andrew Murphy, But the Irish Sea Betwixt Us (1999)

trials.prompt: What is the structural purpose of the Boy's speech (lines 23–44)?
options: To introduce a child character who will survive the play

To provide a clear-eyed, adult-level critique of Pistol/Bardolph/Nym from below — delivered in the voice of a child the play will later permit to be killed offstage. The audience's only fully honest narrator is disposable
To translate Fluellen's Welsh for the audience

trials.prompt: Macmorris's line 'What ish my nation? Who talks of my nation?' is famous because —
options: It is a nonsense line inserted for comedy only

Macmorris is Shakespeare's only Irish character; the line has become the most-cited Renaissance-drama passage in Irish studies, staging the contested nature of emergent British identity at the moment of its formation
It is a quotation from Holinshed
It is the longest line in the play

trials.prompt: Fluellen's repeated phrase 'the disciplines of the war' signals what?
options: A serious critique of Henry's strategy
His pedantic preoccupation with classical military theory — especially the 'Roman' and 'pristine' wars he keeps citing; the catchphrase becomes a running comic signature
